narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid upper-middle-class family background, you've put significant effort into your law school journey, including scoring a 37 on the LSAT—which is right around the typical score for students in our dataset—and planning to attend full-time, just like the vast majority of applicants. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated your probability of failing at 68.9%, leading to the unfortunate rejection of your application. This prediction is based on a careful analysis of key factors from your profile compared to thousands of past students. The most influential factor pushing toward a failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, which is notably lower than the average of about 5.5, suggesting your performance in later undergraduate years fell short of what typically leads to bar success. Your Year 1 decile of 3, also below the average of around 5.7, reinforced this concern as the next biggest influence in the same direction. On a positive note, your LSAT score stood out strongly, pulling the prediction toward success more than any other factor, as it exceeded what many similar students achieved. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.8, slightly below the typical 3.2, added a modest push toward failure, while your family income bracket and full-time status provided small boosts toward passing, aligning with common successful profiles. Overall, your academic deciles in Years 1 and 3 were the dominant reasons the model leaned toward rejection, as they deviated more from the stronger rankings seen in students who passed the bar. Compared to typical applicants who get approved, your later-year performance ranks in the lower deciles, which the model flags as a higher risk despite your competitive LSAT. We understand this is disappointing, especially given your strengths, and it doesn't define your potential. To move forward, consider retaking courses or pursuing a post-baccalaureate program to boost your decile rankings, while leveraging that strong LSAT in appeals or alternative law programs that weigh test scores heavily. Many students in similar situations have succeeded by addressing these academic gaps—keep pushing, and we're rooting for you.
